name: New Hampshire
tagline: A lean $15.9B biennial budget, a fresh 2025–2029 statewide IT strategy built around 'Digital Transformation is the Business,' and a rising wave of local-government cyberattacks — New Hampshire is modernizing under tight fiscal discipline.

execSummary.summary: New Hampshire is a small, fiscally conservative, centralized-IT state — a high-touch, relationship-driven market rather than a big-dollar one. Governor Kelly Ayotte signed a $15.9B two-year budget (HB1/HB2) on June 27, 2025, that trimmed agencies (including a roughly $51M cut to Health & Human Services and reductions to the University System), so technology spend will favor consolidation, modernization, and security over net-new expansion. Three things shape the motion for a Dell seller. First, the Department of Information Technology (DoIT) runs a fully consolidated, agency-wide IT model and just ratified its 2025–2029 statewide IT strategy ('Digital Transformation is the Business') in December 2025. Second, cybersecurity is the dominant theme: a string of 2025 ransomware and breach incidents hit counties, courts, and school districts, and the state is pushing a 'whole-of-state' security posture under new CISO Pamela McLeod. Third, buying runs through the DAS Bureau of Purchase & Property statewide contracts, with DoIT approval required on IT purchases over $500.
opportunities: Whole-of-state cybersecurity — endpoint, identity, backup/recovery, and resilience for state agencies plus counties, courts, and K-12 hit by 2025 attacks.
Infrastructure modernization and data-center consolidation under DoIT's 2025–2029 strategy (PowerEdge, storage, networking, services).
Client/endpoint lifecycle refresh across a fully consolidated state IT estate that DoIT manages centrally.
Research-computing and AI infrastructure at the University of New Hampshire (HPC, NSF/NVIDIA-funded multimodal AI work).

budget.intro: New Hampshire budgets on a two-year (biennial) cycle running July 1 to June 30. The FY2026–27 budget — HB1 (operating) and HB2 (trailer/policy) — totals roughly $15.9B and was signed by Governor Ayotte on June 27, 2025. It is a constrained budget that trimmed several agencies, so there is no large standalone 'state IT budget' line; technology spend is centralized under DoIT and distributed across agency operating budgets, with security and modernization prioritized over expansion.

breakdown.label: Federal Broadband (BEAD/BIL)
breakdown.note: Federal funds via Office of Broadband Initiatives

notes: There is no published single statewide 'IT budget' number — IT is consolidated under DoIT and funded through agency operating lines.
Broadband dollars are predominantly federal (BEAD/BIL) and may face federal program changes — confirm current status before forecasting.

initiatives.name: 2025–2029 Statewide IT Strategy
initiatives.summary: The NH IT Council ratified the statewide 2025–2029 IT Strategy & Implementation plan in December 2025 under the theme 'Digital Transformation is the Business.' It sets the mission, goals, and actions driving technology across state government — the blueprint for infrastructure modernization, service delivery, and security investment over the biennium. Aligning Dell offerings to its goals is the way into multi-year planning.

initiatives.name: Whole-of-State Cybersecurity
initiatives.summary: 2025 brought a wave of incidents — Hillsborough County systems offline, Strafford County court disruptions, and ransomware/breaches at Pelham/Hudson school districts and Coös County health services. The state's 2025 and 2026 Cyber Threat Assessments push a 'whole-of-state' posture spanning state, county, and local entities. Under new CISO Pamela McLeod, this is the strongest near-term security tailwind: endpoint, identity, backup/recovery, and resilience.

initiatives.name: Broadband Expansion (BEAD / BIL)
initiatives.summary: New Hampshire received roughly $196.6M in federal broadband funds and its BEAD Final Proposal was approved by NTIA in November 2025; the state has also awarded ~$90M across two rounds to connect ~48,000 homes/businesses by end of 2026, run by the Office of Broadband Initiatives within Business & Economic Affairs. Mostly a connectivity build, but it expands the digital surface DoIT must secure and support.

initiatives.name: Responsible AI Use Policy
initiatives.summary: DoIT maintains a statewide policy on AI and Automated Decision Systems setting principles for responsible, ethical use across agencies, with strong emphasis on protecting Personal Identifying Information (PII). NH is taking a measured, governance-first approach rather than a funded AI program — an opening to lead with governed, private/on-prem AI for sensitive state data rather than open public models.

procurement.intro: Procurement is centralized. The Department of Administrative Services (DAS) Bureau of Purchase & Property runs most state goods and non-construction services through statewide contracts, and most buys post there rather than agency-direct. The Department of Information Technology (DoIT) is the central technology authority: it coordinates, reviews, and approves statewide IT activities, and any IT equipment purchase over $500 requires DoIT review and a DoIT approval number.

vehicles.name: DAS Statewide Contracts (Bureau of Purchase & Property)
vehicles.detail: Primary vehicle for state goods and services, including IT. Confirm Dell and reseller coverage is on current statewide contracts (e.g., software value-added reseller and hardware vehicles).

vehicles.name: DoIT IT approval (>$500)
vehicles.detail: Not a contract — a required gate. IT equipment purchases over $500 must carry a DoIT approval number; engage DoIT early to align purchases with the statewide strategy.

vehicles.name: Cooperative / NASPO ValuePoint
vehicles.detail: NH participates in cooperative and NASPO ValuePoint vehicles for certain categories — useful where a statewide contract does not exist.

entities.name: DoIT
entities.role: Central, consolidated IT authority — owns statewide strategy, approvals, infrastructure, and cybersecurity. The single most important account.

entities.name: DAS Bureau of Purchase & Property
entities.role: Runs statewide contracts and the procurement process for nearly all state buying.

entities.name: Health & Human Services (DHHS)
entities.role: Largest agency and a data-heavy modernization buyer, despite this biennium's cut.

entities.name: University System of New Hampshire (UNH)
entities.role: Independent research-computing, HPC, and AI-infrastructure buyer with its own ET&S IT organization.

procurement.competition: New Hampshire is a small, centralized, relationship-driven market where price discipline and statewide-contract presence matter more than scale. Because DoIT consolidates IT and gates purchases, the winning motion is fewer, deeper relationships — aligning to the 2025–2029 strategy and the whole-of-state cyber agenda — rather than chasing many independent agency deals.

powerStructure.name: Kelly Ayotte
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Signed the $15.9B FY2026–27 budget June 27, 2025; fiscal-discipline agenda; backs broadband expansion.

powerStructure.name: Denis Goulet
powerStructure.title: Commissioner & State CIO
powerStructure.note: Long-tenured CIO (since 2015); recognized by NASCIO in 2025 for a decade of service. Owns statewide IT direction.

powerStructure.name: Pamela McLeod
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
powerStructure.note: Took the role April 2025 from ACT and the Concord School District; leads the whole-of-state cyber push.

powerStructure.name: NH Information Technology Council
powerStructure.title: IT governance body
powerStructure.note: Ratified the 2025–2029 statewide IT strategy in Dec 2025; sets technology direction across agencies.

news.title: NH on track to end SFY2026 with a revenue surplus
news.impact: After a $67.3M FY2025 deficit covered by the Rainy Day Fund, the state is now expected to close the fiscal year (June 30, 2026) with a General/Education Trust Fund surplus — a modest fiscal turnaround that gives more room for the FY2028–29 biennial budget (Governor's proposal due Feb 2027). Still a price-disciplined market, but a slightly more favorable backdrop for modernization and cyber spend.

news.title: Data-center and AI bills stall in the legislature
news.impact: SB 439 (statewide data-center framework) was tabled 304–11 in May 2026, the HB 1265 moratorium and HB 1722 rate-class bills failed, and the 'Right to Compute' bill (HB 1124) and an AI-regulation bill (HB 1725) were sent to interim study / killed — NH lands no statewide data-center or AI framework this session, keeping the Dell motion on state-government modernization and cyber rather than data-center ecosystem.

news.title: Colebrook town disconnected from state systems after email compromise
news.impact: A hacked town email account led NH to suspend Colebrook's connections to state systems (vital records, elections, DMV, dispatch) to stop lateral spread — a vivid whole-of-state cyber data point reinforcing demand for identity, email security, segmentation, and recovery at the local-to-state boundary.

news.title: IT Council ratifies 2025–2029 statewide IT strategy
news.impact: 'Digital Transformation is the Business' becomes the blueprint for modernization and security spend — align Dell offerings to its goals.

news.title: NTIA approves New Hampshire's BEAD Final Proposal
news.impact: Unlocks federal broadband execution (~$196.6M total federal); expands the digital footprint DoIT must support and secure.

news.title: Governor Ayotte signs $15.9B FY2026–27 budget (HB1/HB2)
news.impact: A constrained budget with agency cuts; technology spend favors consolidation, modernization, and security over expansion.

dellActions: Make DoIT the anchor account: map Dell ISG, client, and services offerings to the 2025–2029 statewide IT strategy and engage on modernization/consolidation planning, not one-off buys.

Lead with whole-of-state cybersecurity — endpoint, identity, immutable backup/recovery, and resilience for state plus the counties, courts, and K-12 districts hit in 2025; build the relationship with CISO Pamela McLeod's team.
Confirm Dell and reseller coverage on current DAS statewide contracts, and bake the DoIT >$500 IT-approval step into every deal path so it doesn't stall at close.
Engage the University of New Hampshire independently for HPC and AI-infrastructure (NSF/NVIDIA-funded research computing) outside the central state-agency motion.
Position governed, private/on-prem AI for PII-sensitive workloads consistent with DoIT's responsible AI-use policy, rather than open public-model approaches.
